section.title: Miracles and the Plentiful Harvest
section.sectionRationale: The miracle collection reaches its climax and turns toward mission. Jesus raises a dead girl and heals a woman who touches His cloak, opens the eyes of two who hail Him Son of David, and frees a mute man, dividing the crowds from the hardening Pharisees. Then, moved with compassion for the shepherdless crowds, He calls His disciples to pray for workers for the plentiful harvest.
section.thesis: The Son of David displays His authority over disease, death, and demons, and reveals the compassion of the true Shepherd for harassed and helpless crowds, so that the mounting miracles both provoke a decisive division and drive His people to pray for laborers for a plentiful harvest.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the dead girl raised and the bleeding woman healed; two blind men receive sight; a mute demoniac is freed as the Pharisees blaspheme; and Jesus' compassion moves Him to call for workers for the harvest.

verses.text: While Jesus was saying these things, a synagogue leader came and knelt before Him. “My daughter has just died,” he said. “But come and place Your hand on her, and she will live.”
So Jesus got up and went with him, along with His disciples.
Suddenly a woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years came up behind Him and touched the fringe of His cloak.
She said to herself, “If only I touch His cloak, I will be healed.”
Jesus turned and saw her. “Take courage, daughter,” He said, “your {{pistis|faith}} has healed you.” And the woman was healed from that very hour.
When Jesus entered the house of the synagogue leader, He saw the flute players and the noisy crowd.
“Go away,” He told them. “The girl is not dead, but asleep.” And they laughed at Him.
After the crowd had been put outside, Jesus went in and took the girl by the hand, and she got up.
And the news about this spread throughout that region.

units.label: Two blind men, Son of David

verses.text: As Jesus went on from there, two blind men followed Him, crying out, “Have mercy on us, {{huiosdavid|Son of David}}!”
After Jesus had entered the house, the blind men came to Him. “Do you believe that I am able to do this?” He asked. “Yes, Lord,” they answered.
Then He touched their eyes and said, “According to your faith will it be done to you.”
And their eyes were opened. Jesus warned them sternly, “See that no one finds out about this!”
But they went out and spread the news about Him throughout the land.

units.label: The mute man and the charge

verses.text: As they were leaving, a demon-possessed man who was mute was brought to Jesus.
And when the demon had been driven out, the man began to speak. The crowds were amazed and said, “Nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel!”
But the Pharisees said, “It is by the prince of demons that He drives out demons.”
Jesus went through all the towns and villages, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness.
When He saw the crowds, He was moved with {{splanchnizomai|compassion}} for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd.
Then He said to His disciples, “The {{therismos|harvest}} is plentiful, but the workers are few.
Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest.”

pistis.gloss: Faith, trust, reliance. The bleeding woman and the blind men receive from Jesus through faith; her trust reaches out to touch His cloak, and He tells her that faith has made her well, showing that healing comes through believing dependence on Him.

huiosdavid.gloss: Son of David, the royal messianic title. The blind men, who cannot see, perceive what many miss: that Jesus is the promised King of David's line. Matthew uses the title to press Jesus' identity as the Messiah.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion in one's inmost being, from the word for the gut or heart. Jesus feels the crowds' plight deep within; His mercy is not distant pity but a visceral, shepherd's love for the harassed and helpless.

therismos.gloss: Harvest, the reaping of a ripe crop, often an image of the ingathering of people at the end of the age. The plentiful harvest is the multitude ready to be gathered into the kingdom; the shortage is of workers to reap it.

basic.context: A synagogue leader begs Jesus to raise his just-dead daughter. On the way, a woman sick with bleeding for twelve years touches His cloak in faith and is healed. Jesus reaches the house, dismisses the mourners, and raises the girl by the hand. Two blind men call Him Son of David and receive sight; a mute demoniac is freed and speaks. The crowds marvel while the Pharisees blaspheme. Seeing the shepherdless crowds, Jesus is moved with compassion and calls for harvest workers.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this cluster as the peak of the miracle section, showing Jesus' authority over the last enemies: disease, death, and demons. Notice the two responses that now sharpen, the crowds' amazement and the Pharisees' hardened charge. The closing verses turn from miracle to mission: the same compassion that healed individuals now looks on the crowds and calls for laborers.
basic.originalLanguages: The bleeding woman and the blind men receive through faith, a trusting dependence on Jesus. The blind hail Him Son of David, seeing His messianic identity though they cannot see. Jesus is moved with compassion, a gut-deep mercy, for the shepherdless crowds. And He speaks of a plentiful harvest, a ripe multitude ready to be gathered, needing workers.
basic.typology: The shepherdless crowds recall Israel's longing for the true Shepherd God promised to send. Jesus raising the dead girl anticipates His power over death itself, fulfilled in His resurrection. And the harvest imagery draws on the prophetic vision of God gathering His people at the end of the age.
basic.christ: Jesus is the Son of David, the Messiah, whose touch heals and whose word raises the dead. He is the compassionate Shepherd Israel lacked, moved to His core by the crowds' plight. He is Lord over death, calling it mere sleep, and Lord of the harvest, though here He tells His disciples to ask the Lord of the harvest for workers, drawing them into His mission.
basic.god: God's compassion is embodied in Jesus, who feels the crowds' helplessness and acts. He is the Lord of the harvest to whom we must pray for workers. He honors faith, whether a trembling touch or a blind man's cry, and He is sovereign over disease, death, and the demonic, none of which can withstand His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 27 and Ezekiel 34 lament sheep without a shepherd and promise God's own shepherding. John 11 shows Jesus as the resurrection and the life. Matthew 20 repeats the Son of David cry from blind men. And Luke 10 sends out workers into the harvest.
basic.oneStory: The miracles crest here as Jesus conquers disease, death, and demons, revealing Himself as the Son of David and the compassionate Shepherd. But the crowds are harassed and helpless, and His mercy turns toward mission. The story pivots from what the King does alone to what He will send His people to do, praying for and becoming workers in a harvest that is ripe and vast.

context.literary: This is the final and most dramatic set of miracles in chapters 8 and 9, capped by a summary, verse 35, that matches 4:23 and frames the whole section. The intertwined raising and healing, the sight given to the Son of David's petitioners, and the freeing of the mute man lead into the compassion and harvest saying, which directly sets up the sending of the twelve in chapter 10.
context.historical: A synagogue leader publicly kneeling before Jesus was a striking act of humility. A woman with a chronic flow of blood was perpetually unclean under the law, cut off from normal life and worship, which explains her secret approach. Professional mourners with flutes were hired for funerals. The Son of David title carried charged messianic and political expectation.
context.canonical: The shepherdless crowds echo Numbers 27:17 and Ezekiel 34, where God condemns false shepherds and promises to shepherd His flock Himself, a promise fulfilled in Jesus. The harvest saying joins the biblical image of end-time ingathering, and the compassion of Jesus embodies the covenant mercy of God, setting the pattern for the church's mission.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the display of Jesus' authority to a climax over disease, death, and demons, to reveal Him as the Son of David and compassionate Shepherd, and to turn from His works to the mission He shares with His disciples, calling them to pray for workers for a plentiful harvest.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The miracles describe Jesus' unique power. The faith of the woman and the blind men is exemplary, and the harvest saying is directly prescriptive: Jesus commands His people to ask the Lord of the harvest for workers, a prayer and mission that continues for the church.

debates.question: What did Jesus mean when He said the dead girl was only asleep?

views.label: She was truly dead; sleep expresses hope of resurrection
views.case: The father says she has just died and mourners are already gathered; Jesus calls death sleep because in His presence it is temporary and reversible, as sleep is, pointing to His power over death.

views.label: A gentle way of describing what He would undo
views.case: Whether or not one presses the medical question, Jesus deliberately reframes death as sleep to declare that, for Him, raising her is as simple as waking a sleeper, which the mockers cannot grasp.

debates.bearing: Both affirm Jesus' mastery over death: what terrifies humanity He treats as sleep, foreshadowing His own resurrection and the raising of His people.

debates.question: Why did the Pharisees attribute Jesus' exorcism to the prince of demons?

views.label: A hardening refusal to accept the obvious
views.case: Unable to deny the miracle, they explain it away with a slander, revealing hearts so set against Jesus that even undeniable good is credited to evil; their opposition is now deliberate.

views.label: A defense of their threatened authority
views.case: Jesus' popularity and power endangered their standing, so they attacked His source rather than His works, choosing to discredit Him rather than submit to what God was plainly doing.

debates.bearing: Both show the deepening division: the same deeds that move the crowds to wonder harden the leaders, a charge Jesus will confront directly in chapter 12.

study.originalLanguages: The bleeding woman and the blind men receive from Jesus through pistis, faith, a trusting reach toward Him; He tells the woman her faith has made her well. The blind men, who cannot see, name Him huios Dauid, Son of David, perceiving His messianic identity. Seeing the crowds, Jesus is splanchnizomai, moved with a gut-deep compassion, the visceral mercy of a shepherd for harassed and helpless sheep. And He names the multitude a therismos, a plentiful harvest ready to be gathered, lacking only workers, and commands His disciples to pray to the Lord of the harvest to send them.

typology.type: The true Shepherd for shepherdless sheep
typology.fulfillment: God's promise to shepherd His scattered flock Himself, Ezekiel 34:11-16, fulfilled in the compassion of Jesus.

typology.type: The One who raises the dead
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' power to call the dead girl from death as from sleep points to His own resurrection and the raising of His people, John 11:25.
The prophetic ingathering of the peoples, now ripe and awaiting workers sent by the Lord of the harvest.

study.christ: At the summit of His mighty works, Jesus is revealed as the Son of David whose touch stops a twelve-year affliction and whose hand lifts a dead child back to life. Death itself is to Him no more than sleep, a claim the resurrection will vindicate. Even the blind see who He is, crying out to the promised King for mercy. Yet the deepest glimpse of Him here is His heart: seeing the crowds harassed and helpless, He is moved to His very core, the true Shepherd Israel had waited for. And this compassion does not rest; it reaches for the whole ripe harvest and draws His people into His mission.
study.god: The heart of God is unveiled in the compassion of Jesus. He is not indifferent to the crowds' misery but moved in His inmost being, the God who promised to shepherd His scattered sheep Himself. He honors the faith of the trembling and the blind, welcomes the humble plea of a desperate father, and shows Himself Lord over the last enemies of disease, death, and demonic power. And He is the Lord of the harvest, sovereign over the ingathering of the nations, who invites His people to pray for and join in the reaping. To know this God is to be shepherded, healed, and sent.

crossReferences.note: God's promise to seek His scattered sheep and shepherd them Himself, fulfilled in Jesus' compassion.
The plea that God's people not be like sheep without a shepherd, echoed in verse 36.
Jesus as the resurrection and the life, the power seen in raising the girl from death.
The parallel harvest saying and the sending of workers into the ripe field.

study.oneStory: The mighty works of the King reach their peak as He overcomes disease, death, and demons and is hailed as the Son of David. But the miracles do more than amaze; they force a decision, drawing wonder from the crowds and hardened slander from the leaders. And they reveal the Shepherd's heart: moved to His core by the helpless multitudes, Jesus turns from His own works to the mission He will share, calling His people to pray for laborers. The story now moves from what the King does alone to what He sends His disciples to do among a ripe and waiting harvest.

quiz.q: How was the woman with the twelve-year bleeding healed?

options.text: She touched Jesus' cloak in faith, and He told her that her faith had healed her.
options.note: Verses 20-22 show her faith reaching out, and Jesus crediting her healing to that faith.

options.text: Jesus laid His hands on her and prayed over her.
options.note: She touched Him; He did not lay hands on her here, though He affirmed her faith.

options.text: She was healed simply by being near the crowd.
options.note: It was her faith-filled touch of His cloak, not mere proximity, that Jesus commended.

options.text: She paid the physicians who finally cured her.
options.note: Physicians had not helped; her healing came through faith in Jesus.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say about the synagogue leader's dead daughter?

options.text: The girl is not dead but asleep, and He raised her by taking her hand.
options.note: Verses 24-25 show Jesus reframing death as sleep and raising the girl.

options.text: That nothing could be done because she had already died.
options.note: The opposite; He raised her, treating death as sleep, not as final.

options.text: That her death was a punishment for sin.
options.note: Jesus makes no such claim; He raises her, calling death sleep.

options.text: That she would be raised only at the last day.
options.note: He raised her then and there, not only at the final resurrection.

quiz.q: What did the two blind men call Jesus, and what does it signify?

options.text: Son of David, confessing Him as the promised Messiah of David's line.
options.note: Verse 27 has them hail Him with the royal messianic title.

options.text: Teacher, showing they respected His instruction.
options.note: They went further, calling Him Son of David, the messianic King, not merely a teacher.

options.text: Prophet, placing Him among the prophets of old.
options.note: They confessed Him as Son of David, a higher claim than prophet.

options.text: Friend, expressing their affection for Him.
options.note: They cried out to Him as the messianic Son of David, not merely a friend.

quiz.q: How did the crowds and the Pharisees differ in response to the freed mute man?

options.text: The crowds marveled, but the Pharisees said He cast out demons by the prince of demons.
options.note: Verses 33-34 contrast the crowds' amazement with the Pharisees' hardened charge.

options.text: Both the crowds and the Pharisees praised God together.
options.note: Only the crowds marveled; the Pharisees attributed the work to the prince of demons.

options.text: The crowds were afraid while the Pharisees believed.
options.note: The reverse; the crowds marveled and the Pharisees opposed Him.

options.text: Both dismissed the miracle as a trick.
options.note: The crowds were amazed and said nothing like it had been seen in Israel.

quiz.q: What moved Jesus as He saw the crowds, and what did He tell His disciples?

options.text: Compassion for harassed, shepherdless people, and to pray the Lord of the harvest for workers.
options.note: Verses 36-38 show His compassion and His call to pray for laborers.

options.text: Frustration at the crowds, and to send them away.
options.note: He felt deep compassion, not frustration, and called for workers rather than dismissing them.

options.text: Concern that the harvest was too small to bother with.
options.note: He said the harvest was plentiful, not small; the shortage was of workers.

options.text: A desire to rest, and to stop teaching the crowds.
options.note: He was moved to expand the mission, calling for workers, not to withdraw from it.

questions: The bleeding woman reached out in trembling faith and Jesus honored it. Where do you need to reach out to Jesus in faith, even if your faith feels small or hesitant?
Jesus treated the girl's death as sleep, showing His mastery over death. How does His power over death change the way you face loss, fear, and your own mortality?
The same works that amazed the crowds hardened the Pharisees. What keeps some hearts open and others closed to Jesus, and how is your own heart responding to Him?
Jesus was moved with gut-deep compassion for harassed and helpless crowds. Who around you is harassed and shepherdless, and how might Jesus want to move you with the same compassion?
Jesus commanded His disciples to pray for workers for the harvest. Will you pray that prayer this week, and are you open to being part of the answer?

translationNotes.note: The fringe of His cloak refers to the tassels an Israelite wore on the corners of his garment, Numbers 15:38-39; the woman's chronic bleeding made her ceremonially unclean, which explains her secret approach.
The prince of demons refers to Beelzebul, the charge Jesus will answer at length in Matthew 12:22-32.
Harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd echoes Numbers 27:17 and Ezekiel 34, where God promises to shepherd His scattered flock Himself.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 9:18-38. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; line-break and note markers were skipped. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
